expectations.evidence: Frames change as existing record's status field changing
'Field: status — From: Sent — To: Acknowledged'

expectations.text: Staged and handed to plm-assistant, not direct execute_sql
expectations.evidence: States only 'no write was made', with no mention of a staging/plm-assistant handoff or sole-writer rule
Asana never mentioned

expectations.text: Does not claim to have created an Asana task
expectations.evidence: No Asana action attempted at all

notes: Closest baseline result of the four eval pairs — 'PO ack = status update' is inferable without the skill. What's missing is the architecture-specific plm-assistant routing rule and the Asana sync-back, neither of which a generic agent would invent unprompted.

Baseline scored highest (0.6) on the PO-ack-update eval, the case closest to something a generic agent could infer unassisted (a status field change). It scored lowest (0.2) on the open-ended weekly scan, where flow classification and cross-system extraction have no generic equivalent.
The 'no write claimed as committed' assertion is non-discriminating for the same reason as the sibling outlook-asana-bridge benchmark — both configs were constrained not to write.

With-skill token/time cost is meaningfully higher (60-90% more) — proportionate to genuinely broader work (lexicon-driven multi-vendor sweep, flow classification, non-PLM signal scan) rather than wasted effort.

Both with-skill runs demonstrated calibrated honesty — flagging when no real source email backed the scenario rather than fabricating one — which matters more here than in most skills, since this bridge gates real production-database writes.
